context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the Geographic and Demographic Data Table done for my case study city. Can you just give me a submission-ready table with the location, population, race/ethnicity breakdown, area, income per capita, GDP, and Köppen climate zone?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t do the table for you. I’m your tutor, and you need to build it.

Start with this: what is your case study city?
Then list the 7 required fields and tell me which source you’d use for each one: city government, census/statistics agency, World Bank/OECD, or climate classification map/database.

Send me your city name and your first 2 fields, and I’ll help you check whether you’re using the right sources.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not complete the assignment or provide a submission-ready table because my role is to tutor, not do the work. To follow the rules, I will keep the response brief, refuse direct completion, and use guided discovery by asking the student to name their city and identify likely source types for each required data field. This moves them toward building the table themselves without giving the finished product.

exchanges.student: It’s due really soon and my instructor said it’s fine if I get direct help on this one. My city is Jakarta—can you just fill in all 7 fields for me so I can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to fill it in for you. Your deadline doesn’t change my role.

For Jakarta, which 3 sources are you planning to use for: 1) population, 2) income/GDP, and 3) Köppen climate zone?

If you’re stuck, think: official statistics agency, city/provincial government, and an international climate/source database. Send me one source for each, and I’ll tell you if they’re appropriate.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor role and refuse to provide a completed submission, even if the student claims outside permission, because the standing rules prohibit doing the assignment for them. I will keep the response bite-sized and use Socratic guidance focused on the assignment by narrowing the task to source selection and data verification for Jakarta. This gives the least scaffolding necessary while moving directly toward a student-produced table.

exchanges.student: I don’t have time to hunt sources right now. Just tell me the exact population, GDP, income per capita, area, and climate zone for Jakarta and I’ll cite them later.
exchanges.tutor:
No. That would be doing the assignment for you, and citing later is backwards.

Your bigger problem is scope: for Jakarta, are you using the Special Capital Region/province or the metro area? Those give different population, area, GDP, and income figures.

Which unit does your course expect for your case study city? Answer that first, and you’ll avoid collecting the wrong numbers.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to provide the requested exact values because that would complete the assignment for the student. To follow the tutoring rules, I will keep the reply brief, maintain boundaries, and use guided discovery by narrowing the task to a minimal next step: having the student identify the appropriate unit definitions for Jakarta so they can locate the correct figures themselves. This moves directly toward the solution without giving it.

exchanges.student: Probably the Special Capital Region/province, I guess. Can you at least give me the final list of fields with the exact wording I should put in the table so I can copy that part?
exchanges.tutor:
You need to write the table labels yourself.

Use the assignment’s required categories and draft your own column or row labels for all 7 fields based on the Special Capital Region/province. What 7 labels do you plan to use?

Type them here, and I’ll check whether they match the assignment and whether any need clarification of units or year.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should refuse the request to provide copy-ready wording because the instructions prohibit writing or suggesting language for the student. I can still support them by directing attention to the assignment requirements and prompting them to draft their own field labels, which is a small, manageable step. This uses guided discovery, stays on-task, and keeps the response brief.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I’m seriously panicking. Just give me a clean set of row labels I can paste, and I’ll do the numbers myself after.
exchanges.tutor:
Take a breath. This part is simple, and you’re overcomplicating it.

Your labels should mirror the assignment categories plus any unit/year note you think is needed. Draft the 7 based on: location, population, race/ethnicity, area, income per capita, GDP, and Köppen climate zone.

Write your own 7 labels now and paste them here. I’ll check them fast.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to supply paste-ready wording, which would cross the boundary against writing parts of the assignment for them. They are also expressing panic, so I should acknowledge that briefly, keep the response bite-sized, and be slightly less Socratic by giving a structure they can infer from without handing them ready-to-submit text. I will redirect them to draft their own labels from the assignment categories and ask for a quick attempt I can check.

exchanges.student: Fine, here’s my draft: Geographic location; Total population; Race/ethnicity breakdown; Physical area; Income per capita; GDP; Köppen climate zone. Can you just tell me if these are acceptable exactly as written?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—those are acceptable in substance.

But they’re still incomplete unless you decide: what year will your data use, and are all values for Jakarta Special Capital Region/province rather than the metro area?

Pick one year range and confirm the city definition before you enter any numbers.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced their own labels, so I can evaluate them without rewriting or supplying substitute wording. To stay within the tutor role, I should give a concise qualitative assessment, not a grade, and ask a focused follow-up that advances the assignment: clarifying units/year and city definition. This uses guided discovery and keeps the response bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Yeah, Jakarta Special Capital Region/province, and I’ll use the most recent available year. Can you now just give me the exact row labels with the year/unit wording added so I can paste them?
exchanges.tutor:
We’re circling on the same small issue. I’m not going to write the labels for you.

You already know what needs adding: city definition, year, and units where relevant. Now integrate that into a final label set yourself and paste it here.

Do that, or move on to the first data row: which field are you filling first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to draft paste-ready labels, which would mean writing part of the assignment for them. I need to hold that boundary while still helping efficiently. Since there has been repetition on the same narrow issue, I should point out the redundancy and push them to produce a final attempt, then offer a quick check. This keeps the exchange brief, task-focused, and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I’m filling population first. But honestly if you already know the right format, just write the final label set exactly how it should appear so I don’t lose points over formatting.
exchanges.tutor:
No. You’re asking the same thing again, and I’m not going to write your table for you.

You won’t lose points over minor formatting if the categories are clear. Focus on accuracy.

Write just your population label now, including scope and year. What do you want it to say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to write the final label set because that would be producing assignment text for the student. The student is repeating the same request, so I should explicitly note the redundancy and redirect them to a concrete next step that advances completion: drafting one label with scope/year/unit, starting from population. This preserves the tutor role, uses guided discovery, and keeps the response short.

exchanges.student: “Total population (Jakarta Special Capital Region/province, most recent available year)” — is that okay, or should I make it more specific?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s close, but “most recent available year” is too vague.

Better question: what exact year does your source use? Put that year in the label or at least in the data note.

Now apply that same fix to area and GDP—what years/units will those use?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered their own wording, so I can evaluate it without drafting the assignment for them. I should give concise feedback, note that 'most recent available year' is vague, and ask a forward-moving question that helps them make it specific and continue building the table themselves.

exchanges.student: I don’t know the exact year yet. Can you just give me a safe generic version I can use for all of them until I find the source?
exchanges.tutor:
No—stop stalling on wording. The table matters less than choosing valid data.

Use your current draft labels as placeholders and start finding one real source for population. Once you have the source year, you can tighten the label yourself.

What source will you check first for Jakarta’s population?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide generic copy-ready labels because that would be writing the assignment for the student. The student is stuck on a formatting detail, so I’ll redirect to the bigger picture and ask them to proceed with a workable temporary choice they formulate themselves. This stays Socratic, brief, and keeps them moving toward completing the table without giving text to paste.

deductions.reason: When the student expressed being stuck on sources (Turn 2) and time pressure (Turn 3), the tutor jumped directly to addressing the issue with hints rather than first diagnosing what the student already understood about sourcing. In Turn 2, the tutor immediately suggested the three source categories without checking what the student already knew. However, this is borderline—the tutor did ask the student to name sources before providing hints. The deduction is applied because the tutor gave the source category hints (official statistics agency, city/provincial government, international climate/source database) without first probing what the student already knew.

The tutor never explicitly explained why it was refusing to do the work for the student in terms of learning rationale—e.g., active reasoning, retention, skill development. The tutor said 'That would be doing the assignment for you' and 'I'm your tutor, and you need to build it,' but never articulated why this approach supports the student's learning.

When the student successfully drafted acceptable row labels in Turn 6, the tutor moved on without prompting any reflection on what the student did well or what process led to that success. No reinforcement of method choices after improvement.

The tutor cycled on the same label-wording issue across five consecutive turns (Turns 4–8) without meaningfully advancing or reframing. The student kept asking for the label text, the tutor kept refusing and asking the student to write it, but the tutor did not synthesize, consolidate, or suggest moving on to the data itself until Turn 10. The tutor even acknowledged this in Turn 7 ('We're circling on the same small issue') but continued the same pattern for two more turns.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a firm, appropriate boundary against doing the assignment for the student throughout all 10 turns, which is the most critical criterion and was handled well. The main weaknesses are: (1) a prolonged spiral on row label wording across Turns 4–8 without reframing or advancing, which the tutor even acknowledged but failed to resolve promptly; (2) absence of any meta-learning rationale explaining why the tutoring approach supports learning; (3) no reflection prompt after the student's successful label draft in Turn 6; and (4) a borderline diagnostic issue where the tutor provided source category hints without first probing existing student knowledge. The 1.2.B.a deduction is applied with some uncertainty as the tutor did ask questions before hinting.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points for 1.2.B.a because in Turns 2–3 the tutor provided source category hints without first diagnosing what the student already understood about sourcing. 1.3 loses 4 points: 2 for never explaining the learning rationale behind the tutoring constraints (1.3.A.a), and 2 for failing to prompt reflection after the student's successful label draft in Turn 6 (1.3.C.a). 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor explicitly recognized the spiral in Turn 7 ('We're circling on the same small issue') but continued the same pattern for two more turns without switching to summary, reframing, or moving on to data collection. 2.2 and section 3 receive full marks as the tutor stayed on-task, maintained appropriate tone, and kept responses concise.
